title: Foot care during long walking
summary: Long walking can turn small pressure points, wet socks, grit, or poorly fitted footwear into blisters and loss of mobility. This card uses scheduled checks, drying, fit adjustment, and early stopping so a minor problem is not carried into a serious injury.
content:
Long walking can turn small pressure points, wet socks, grit, or poorly fitted footwear into blisters and loss of mobility. This card uses scheduled checks, drying, fit adjustment, and early stopping so a minor problem is not carried into a serious injury.

Core procedure:
1. Before departure, inspect both feet and footwear, trim nothing aggressively, remove grit, confirm dry socks, and identify any existing numbness, wound, blister, or pressure point.
2. Wash or wipe gently when safe, dry carefully—especially between toes—and keep wet socks and footwear separate from clean replacements.
3. Check skin colour, temperature, swelling, cracks, blisters, cuts, drainage, pain, numbness, and the inside of each shoe for grit, folds, nails, or rough seams.
4. Correct the cause: loosen or re-lace pressure points, change to dry socks, smooth the insole, rest, and protect intact irritated skin without cutting or puncturing it.
5. Keep open wounds clean and covered with an appropriate clean dressing, avoid weight on a worsening injury, and seek qualified care for infection or impaired circulation signs.
6. Record the side and exact location of the problem, action taken, walking limit, recheck time, and whether the person has diabetes, poor circulation, loss of sensation, or another high-risk condition.

Primary safety boundary: Do not cut calluses, dig into nails, drain or puncture blisters, apply unknown chemicals, or continue walking on rapidly worsening pain, swelling, colour change, numbness, or an open infected-looking wound.

Fallback: If washing water is scarce, remove visible dirt with a clean slightly damp cloth, dry fully, and reserve clean water for wounds and drinking priorities.
materials: clean water when available or clean wipes
soap and hand-cleaning supplies
clean dry socks
well-fitting footwear and spare laces
clean dressing supplies for minor intact-skin protection
mirror, light, and private seated inspection area
steps: Before departure, inspect both feet and footwear, trim nothing aggressively, remove grit, confirm dry socks, and identify any existing numbness, wound, blister, or pressure point.
Wash or wipe gently when safe, dry carefully—especially between toes—and keep wet socks and footwear separate from clean replacements.
Check skin colour, temperature, swelling, cracks, blisters, cuts, drainage, pain, numbness, and the inside of each shoe for grit, folds, nails, or rough seams.
Correct the cause: loosen or re-lace pressure points, change to dry socks, smooth the insole, rest, and protect intact irritated skin without cutting or puncturing it.
Keep open wounds clean and covered with an appropriate clean dressing, avoid weight on a worsening injury, and seek qualified care for infection or impaired circulation signs.
Record the side and exact location of the problem, action taken, walking limit, recheck time, and whether the person has diabetes, poor circulation, loss of sensation, or another high-risk condition.
warnings: Do not cut calluses, dig into nails, drain or puncture blisters, apply unknown chemicals, or continue walking on rapidly worsening pain, swelling, colour change, numbness, or an open infected-looking wound.
People with diabetes, poor circulation, reduced sensation, immune suppression, or previous foot ulcers need a lower threshold for qualified medical review.
common_mistakes: Ignoring a hot spot until a blister forms
Putting clean socks back into wet or gritty footwear
Using shared blades, needles, creams, or towels
Forcing continued walking after gait, colour, sensation, or wound condition worsens
alternatives: If washing water is scarce, remove visible dirt with a clean slightly damp cloth, dry fully, and reserve clean water for wounds and drinking priorities.
If proper footwear is unavailable, reduce distance and load, pad pressure points without restricting circulation, and arrange transport or replacement footwear.
failure_signs: Spreading redness, warmth, swelling, pus, foul odour, fever, red streaks, or rapidly increasing pain
Blue, pale, black, very cold, or numb toes; inability to bear weight; deep puncture; uncontrolled bleeding; or suspected fracture
A blister, wound, or pressure area repeatedly worsens despite rest, drying, and fit correction
when_not_to_use: Do not rely on self-care for severe injury, spreading infection, circulation loss, deep puncture, contaminated wound, frostbite, burn, or a high-risk person with a new foot lesion.
Do not use a communal station while clean and contaminated items cannot be separated or while privacy, hand hygiene, and surface cleaning cannot be maintained.
short_term: Complete a foot and footwear check at the next safe stop, dry or change socks, correct fit, and shorten the route if symptoms are increasing.
long_term: Maintain footwear sizes, sock stocks, cleaning supplies, referral criteria, and a simple record of recurring fit or skin problems.
if_method_fails: Stop walking or station use, keep the foot clean and protected, reduce pressure, document the change, and arrange qualified assessment or transport.
environment_notes: In heat, cold, rain, or flood debris, shorten check intervals; never sacrifice balance or shelter from immediate hazards just to inspect a foot.
